note: Curated, human-approved answers drawn from the lab's audited findings. Every entry is a verdict someone signed off on — this is the instant-answer layer. Open-ended questions go to the grounded search assistant (and, if still unanswered, become a research request).

items.q: Can a model warn about unsafe beach water before the lab result?
alt: is the beach safe to swim
items.a: Yes — this is the lab's strongest result. A statewide California nowcast hits ROC-AUC 0.868 / average-precision 0.5099, calibrated and out-of-time stable on the 2022+ holdout, and is deploy-ready in 8 of 9 counties. It beats station-memory, seasonal-naive, and the AB411 rain rule. The culture lab takes 18–24h; this scores a same-day risk before the result lands.

Partly. Rain is a real but modest fresh signal (on the order of +0.01 AP). The backbone of the skill is actually the prior weekly sample plus beach-specific history and season — rain is the one input that is genuinely new at decision time. So rain helps, but it is not the engine.

items.q: Does satellite chlorophyll or turbidity predict bacteria?
items.a: No. Satellite turbidity/chlorophyll is the field's #2 named FIB predictor, but it washes out once the baseline is fair — even slice-conditioned. The only remaining lever on that axis is in-situ (in-water) turbidity, not satellite. Kept on the record so it isn't re-tried blindly.

items.q: Can you forecast bacteria a day ahead, not just nowcast?
items.a: Almost for free, because the skill backbone is the prior weekly sample, which is already forecast-valid. Nowcast→next-day costs little (gated AP ~0.540→0.505). The often-quoted '10×' is lead-time/operational value, not a 10× jump in AP — a real-data leakage gate killed the inflated '+3 days free' version.

items.q: Does the California model transfer to other countries (e.g. Ireland)?
items.a: As a ranker, yes. The CA nowcast transfers zero-shot to Ireland (marine AP ~0.122, ~4.3× over base, CI-separated, permutation-null clean). It ranks dirty days well in a new country with no local labels — but calibration (the exact probability) needs local recalibration. Ranking travels; thresholds don't.

items.q: Does the physics transfer add anything in Australia?
items.a: Pure physics-π transfer does NOT beat Australia's local station memory on its own. But the physics+memory combo is a confirmed augmentation: +0.10 AP (CI [0.043, 0.167]). The physics earns its keep as an addition to local history, not as a replacement.

items.q: Can you predict which clean beaches will suddenly turn dirty?
items.a: Yes, as triage, not an official closure button. Reframing to clean-to-dirty onset, the model beats station-memory on the scoped retrospective gate. The current critic-gated update is: physics-pi raises the onset AP from 0.2992 to 0.3348, while the hardened tensor rerun is not promoted. It tells counties which recently clean beaches to inspect first after rain, heat, or flushing stress.

items.q: Do more data and bigger models beat the simple 'like yesterday' forecast?
items.a: Usually no — this is the lab's default law. Across every hazard tried, piling on weather/river/satellite drivers or a bigger model does NOT beat 'what's happening now plus the time of year.' We treat that as the null and make a model earn its keep. The escapes are specific (slow-driver lead, first-flush onset, spring-neap tide), not general.

items.q: Is the ddPCR molecular test a cleaner label than culture?
items.a: Not 'cleaner' — different. On 909 paired samples, ddPCR and culture disagree ~48% of the time at the official cutoffs, asymmetrically. That points to a different measurement target, not a lower-noise version of the same thing. The frontier is the target definition, and molecular labels remain a real (unbuilt) lever.

items.q: Does your domoic acid forecast beat C-HARM by 10x?
items.a: No 10× — that was a broken-baseline mirage (C-HARM is near-anti-skillful at pier points, AUC ~0.30). The honest lift over naive persistence is ~1.4×. Real, but small; domoic acid is largely persistence-ceilinged.

items.q: Can you triage cyanobacteria blooms across many lakes?
items.a: Yes — shipped. A capture-at-budget allocator over 2,235 lakes adds +4–5.5% onset capture, keyless and deployable, plus a season-ahead per-state watchlist (5–20 weeks). It tells a limited monitoring crew which lakes to sample first.

items.q: Does the onset law replicate in another basin, like Tokyo Bay?
items.a: Yes, but scoped to Tokyo Bay. The supported low-oxygen story is a leakage-guarded Tokyo Bay onset/stratification result: the 7-day onset model reaches AP 0.835 versus 0.747 for the better naive, while the older Gulf survey-cast flag is retained only as an effort-confounded caveat.

items.q: Does tide actually help predict bacteria?
items.a: Yes — a confirmed escape. Harmonic spring-neap tidal range adds +0.011 AP (leave-one-basin-out, permutation-null p=0.02) and is shipped: it moved the statewide headline 0.4992 → 0.5099. One of the few drivers that genuinely beats persistence.

Not yet. The official stranding database is request-walled; iNaturalist dead-annotations reconstruct the 2019–23 gray-whale die-off after the fact, but a forward forecast isn't there. Thermal stratification gives a real hypoxia lead; whale-forage (krill/rockfish) is suggestive but not yet robust. Open lane.

items.q: Is the physics 'interlingua' a universal model that works on any hazard?
items.a: No — it's a shared ENCODING (a dimensionless coordinate system), not a universal model. The hand-built physics-π groups transfer zero-shot within marine-FIB onset across regions, but they go NULL at every cross-hazard jump (FIB→domoic acid, →cyano, →hypoxia). Same measuring stick; different per-signal physics.

items.q: Can you predict San Francisco Bay compound flooding?
items.a: Tested and did not clear the bar. Kept on the record as an honest null so the next experiment doesn't repeat it.
